text: Hello friends. This patient has left-sided eight centimeter endometrioma. But the good thing about that it is a para-ovarian endometrioma. You can see that left side of the ovary

The surgeon uses a harmonic vibrating jaw to puncture the para-ovarian endometrioma, enlarging the opening to insert a suction device. The patient's head is positioned upwards to prevent spilled cyst contents from entering the upper abdomen, and the entire endometrial cyst is aspirated.

The surgeon uses a blunt suction irrigation tip to separate the endometrial cyst adhered to the cul-de-sac from the ovarian fossa. The harmonic device is employed to excise the para-ovarian cyst, with the left hand providing anteromedial traction. The excised cyst is then placed into an endo bag for removal.

The surgeon separates adhesions from the left utero sacral ligament and the ovary adhered in the ovarian fossa. The cyst wall is further excised and completely removed. Suction irrigation is performed to clean the area of oozing and chocolate material from the cyst. Fulguration of the cyst bed is carried out to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon performs fulguration on the ovarian side of the para-ovarian cyst to eliminate any remaining endometriotic tissue and prevent recurrence. The left ovarian fossa, where the ovary was adhered, is also fulgurated using electrosurgery, maintaining a 0.5 millimeter distance to create a spray effect that minimizes depth of injury while covering a wider surface area.

The surgeon uses a spatula for fulguration on the posterior wall of the uterus, cul-de-sac, and uterosacral ligament to treat endometriosis. Following fulguration, repeated suction and irrigation are performed to remove burnt tissue and char, resulting in a clean surgical field with no bleeding or oozing. Interseed is applied to prevent adhesion formation.

The surgeon places a 15x15 interseed into the cul-de-sac, posterior wall of the uterus, and ovarian fossa to prevent adhesion and recurrence of endometriosis. The interseed, resembling a mesh but with tissue paper-like consistency, is carefully spread between the ovary and ovarian fossa, ensuring it does not cover the ovary. The interseed is positioned to cover the complete posterior wall of the uterus and the anterior wall of the rectum.

The surgeon carefully spreads the interseed over the uterosacral ligament, ovary, and ovarian fossa, ensuring the ovary remains posterior to the interseed. Two-hand instruments, including a grasper or Maryland, are used to lift the ovary and position the interseed correctly. The interseed is visibly shredded in the left ovarian fossa where fulguration was previously performed. Once the interseed is properly positioned, the assistant slowly drops the uterus and removes the uterine manipulation device.
The surgeon ensures the interseed is securely adhered around the surgical site, confirming the area is dry with no bleeding or oozing. An endo bag made from gloves is prepared for tissue retrieval.

The surgeon places the removed cyst into an endo bag and uses a 10mm umbilical port to extract the bag. The edges of the glove sleeve are pulled using a semi-traumatic clasp, and the contents are removed with force. After extraction, the port closure is performed using a varus needle due to dilation.

The surgeon uses a varus needle to pierce one side of the wound, excluding the skin, and inserts a loop suture. A grasper pulls the real suture into the loop suture, which then extracts the real suture. The loop suture is used to pull the real suture out, and the knot is tied externally to ensure tight wound closure, preventing hernia at the ten millimeter port site.
This was just a simple case of endometrioma. Have a nice day. God bless you.
